NYC Flights Data

Mia Fitzgerald

4/16/2026

First 6 Origin/Destination Pairs

OD_flights <- flights %>%
  count(origin, dest)

OD_flights %>%
  head(6) %>%
  kable()
origin dest n
EWR ALB 439
EWR ANC 8
EWR ATL 5022
EWR AUS 968
EWR AVL 265
EWR BDL 443

I summarized the massive flights dataset by using count(origin, dest) to tally every unique route and create a frequency column. To keep my report professional, I used head(6) and kable() to transform the raw data into a clean, readable table. 1

XLOOKUP to Join Coordinates

airports_3 <- airports %>% 
  select(lat, lon, faa) 

OD_flights2 <- OD_flights %>%
  left_join(airports_3, join_by(origin == faa)) %>% 
  rename(origin_lat = lat, origin_lon = lon) %>%
  relocate(origin_lat, origin_lon, .before = dest) 

OD_flights2 %>%
  head(6) %>%
  kable()
origin origin_lat origin_lon dest n
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 ALB 439
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 ANC 8
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 ATL 5022
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 AUS 968
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 AVL 265
EWR 40.6925 -74.16867 BDL 443

I refined the airports dataset to isolate only the essential coordinates and unique FAA codes for a more efficient merge. I then used left_join to physically “paste” these coordinates onto my flight data by matching them to the origin airport. After renaming and relocating the columns for better organization, I displayed the first six rows with kable(). 2

First 6 Latitude and Longitude of the Destination Airports

OD_flights3 <- OD_flights %>%
  left_join(airports_3, join_by(dest == faa)) %>% 
  rename(dest_lat = lat, dest_lon = lon) %>%
  relocate(dest_lat, dest_lon, .before = n) 

OD_flights3 %>%
  head(6) %>%
  kable()
origin dest dest_lat dest_lon n
EWR ALB 42.74827 -73.80169 439
EWR ANC 61.17436 -149.99636 8
EWR ATL 33.63672 -84.42807 5022
EWR AUS 30.19453 -97.66989 968
EWR AVL 35.43619 -82.54181 265
EWR BDL 41.93889 -72.68322 443

I did the same thing but for the destination coordinates. 3

Map of USA

ggplot() +
geom_polygon(data = map_data("state"), mapping = aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), fill = NA, color = "gray") +
coord_fixed(1.3)

Imported the ggplot2 package to make a map with guidance code. 4

The Flight Frequency Mapped from NYC

OD_all_coords <- OD_flights3 %>%
  left_join(airports_3, join_by(origin == faa)) %>%
  rename(origin_lat = lat, origin_lon = lon) %>%
  relocate(origin_lat, origin_lon, .before = dest)
  
ggplot() +
geom_polygon(data = map_data("state"), mapping = aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), fill = NA, color = "gray") +
coord_fixed(1.3) +
geom_segment(data = OD_all_coords, mapping = aes(x = origin_lon, y = origin_lat, xend = dest_lon, yend = dest_lat, alpha = n))

I combined the origin and destination coordinates into a single dataset, ensuring each route had a complete set of start and end points. I then use geom_segment to map the actual flight paths across the country. The plot uses the n variable for transparency, which effectively highlights the busiest travel routes. 5

Filtering out Flights Outside the U.S.

OD_all_coords %>% 
  filter(dest_lat > 25 & dest_lat < 50) %>%
ggplot() +
geom_polygon(data = map_data("state"), mapping = aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group), fill = NA, color = "gray") +
coord_fixed(1.3) +
geom_segment(mapping = aes(x = origin_lon, y = origin_lat, xend = dest_lon, yend = dest_lat, alpha = n))

I filtered the dataset to focus exclusively on the continental US, removing long-distance flights that distorted the map’s scale. 6
